insight: Defense Grid is high enough power level that it's worth getting as soon as you have even a small excuse. Compared to alternatives like two Steelsplitters and a Thermite Core, Defense Grid provides immediate absorb (6 absorb, 4 more than a Wall) plus drone generation, whereas the splitters might only deal 4-6 attack in the meantime, which is less valuable than the Defense Grid's immediate impact.
author: mqp
context: mqp was analyzing a game where a player chose Steelsplitters and Thermite Core instead of Defense Grid, arguing Defense Grid was the clearly stronger play.

insight: Redeemer becomes weaker the better the opponent's defense is; Defense Grid is excellent defense and thus counters Redeemer strategies. Additionally, Arka (likely Arkadikos) on the opponent's Defense Grid turn is strong because it promptly blocks the charges that Defense Grid generates.
author: crash_overlord
context: Discussion following mqp's Defense Grid analysis, crash_overlord adding that Redeemer is worse against strong defense and that playing Arka on the opponent's Defense Grid turn can block its charges.

insight: Arms Race functions effectively as 'give each player $8, but force your opponent to spend theirs on Engineers,' which opponents generally don't want. Engineers are particularly inefficient if they sit idle for several turns before providing any block value, making Arms Race especially effective as an early purchase before the opponent needs to soak damage.
author: consensus
context: amalloy, zugz, and others discussing the strategic value and correct use cases for Arms Race.

insight: Arms Race is particularly effective against Antima Comet because it provides attack that can kill the opponent's Engineers, which Antima Comet relies on.
author: namington
context: namington adding to the discussion of Arms Race's best use cases.

insight: Arms Race is very good against frontline rushes, in addition to its anti-Antima Comet applications.
author: f300xen
context: Discussion of Arms Race use cases among multiple players.

insight: Thermite Core is considered the weakest or among the weakest advanced units by top players (mrguy888, redrame), at best providing value equivalent to a build-2 and build-3 Pixie for 1 blue, and rarely even reaching that level of usefulness.
author: consensus
context: Extended community discussion on unit ranking, with mrguy888 and redrame agreeing Thermite Core is a cut below other units.

insight: Thermite Core can have niche value in low-economy games focused on Shredders: when the opponent keeps clicking Shredders and you have low enough attack, you can deny their absorb by clicking Thermite Cores to manipulate your attack total.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy sharing a replay where Thermite Core provided genuine value in a specific low-econ Shredder-heavy game.

insight: Husk is increasingly considered weak because many other units make it irrelevant, and it requires an auto-attack unit to do anything meaningful—a condition that only comes up when those specific other enablers are present.
author: redrame
context: redrame and mrguy888 discussing units at the bottom of power rankings; redrame argues Husk is increasingly irrelevant.

insight: Lucina's first-buyer advantage is significant enough that the unit 'self-balances' less than Centurion; whoever gets Lucina first tends to have a large advantage, whereas Centurion does not confer the same asymmetric first-mover benefit.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of unit power rankings and which units have first-buyer asymmetry.

insight: In Prismata, most games at lower levels are decided by the first 6 turns. Avoiding early mistakes is more impactful than optimizing mid/late-game nuances like tactics, gambits, or conduit transitions—those become relevant only at higher levels where nobody makes early mistakes.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 giving backseating advice to VanitasCabal and generalizing about what matters most at different skill levels.

insight: Community consensus in early 2018 placed apooche and 307th in the top tier of Prismata players, with mrguy888, Redrame, Arkanishu, and Mmsven in the second tier. For learning resources, 307th's article on big red was recommended over Yujiri's unit math article, which was described as both less accurate and more complicated.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 providing a player tier list and recommending resources for new players; redrame partially corroborated the streamer recommendations.

insight: 'Bullet' refers to the 6-seconds-per-turn time setting in Prismata, as clarified by Arkanishu in the context of a proposed bullet tournament.
author: kristian1103
context: jeacaveo asking what a bullet tournament is; Arkanishu clarifying the time control.
